steps.title: Identify the appositive type
steps.content: "Drummer" and "Dennis Chambers" form an appositive pair. Since "Dennis Chambers" is essential for identifying the drummer, it's a restrictive appositive.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content:
"Drummer" is the noun; "Dennis Chambers" names which drummer — a restrictive appositive. Restrictive appositives take no commas.
  • Choice A — trailing comma: "American drummer Dennis Chambers, may be best known..." — a lone trailing comma splits the subject from its verb. ✗
  • Choice B — no punctuation: "American drummer Dennis Chambers may be best known..." — subject flows directly into verb. ✓
  • Choice C — commas both sides: "American drummer, Dennis Chambers, may be best known..." — non-restrictive treatment implies other drummers exist by context; also structurally wrong here. ✗
  • Choice D — comma before: "American drummer, Dennis Chambers may be best known..." — comma after "drummer" severs the appositive from its noun. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
